entities.description: A currently retrievable structural version of the U-M-derived text. Its BODY text is byte-different because of layout but identical after whitespace normalization; it retains 278 BODY DIV1, 436 LG, 16,974 L, 880 PB, and 637 MILESTONE. It changes DLPSTEXTCLASS to ETS/EEBO, flattens two TITLEPAGE structures into FRONT DIV1, renames NOTE1/NOTE2 to NOTE with PLACE values, Q1 to Q, and HI1 to HI, removes EPB/LB and several source attributes, and replaces the legacy availability warning with a public-domain statement.
entities.label: Medictionary current XML for ahb1379
notes: The added IDG value CME00000 is a generic transformed identifier and is not used as the work_id.

access.accessMethod: Written application to the Library; approved researchers may photograph without flash for private study
access.entityLabel: Inner Temple Library, Petyt 511.7, Part VII
access.format: physical manuscript; private researcher photography; paid agency photography by arrangement

localCopies.label: Inner Temple manuscript access and photography page

notes: Written permission is required to publish photographed material; commercial publication is usually charged.

The Library page reports a 1978 World Microfilms project. MEC/MED BIB2350 reports a Bentley Historical Library photostat in MED Records Box 70. Both are collateral access leads only; current availability and reuse terms remain unverified.

rights.basis: The medieval manuscript text predates modern copyright terms.
notes: This status applies only to the historical text, not to photographs, microfilm, photostats, or other modern reproductions.

rights.basis: Inner Temple limits researcher photography to private study and requires written permission to publish photographed material. Copyright and reuse status for the 1978 World Microfilms copy and Bentley Historical Library MED Records Box 70 photostat were not verified.
rights.component: Researcher-created or commissioned photographs and reported modern reproduction layers
notes: Neither collateral reproduction is modeled as Furnivall's source intermediary.

entities.description: A complete manuscript witness of the two-part chronicle. Furnivall uses it for target lines 1-198, exact later supplies, and continuing collation; the target occupies principally fols. 1ra-95va and Part II begins on fol. 95b.

holding.notes: DIMEV records the complete chronicle at fols. 1ra-195.

entities.label: Inner Temple Library, Petyt 511.7, Part VII
notes: No precise production date is normalized in this draft because the directly reviewed access page does not supply one.

Collateral reproduction routes reported by the reviewed sources are the 1978 World Microfilms copy and a MED photostat at Bentley Historical Library, MED Records Box 70. Neither is Furnivall's source intermediary, and current availability or reuse permission was not verified.
Furnivall reports one hand for the target through fol. 95va.

rights.basis: The medieval manuscript text predates modern copyright terms.

notes: Neither collateral reproduction is modeled as Furnivall's source intermediary.

notes: James describes two hands and loss of the first leaf. Furnivall's historical dating is not used over the later catalog.

next_steps: Inspect fol. 71 directly or order the terminal leaves; preserve the current broad through-fol.-71v locator until then.
Do not infer an exact column solely from the next encoded Petyt or Lambeth milestone.
openQuestions.question: What exact Lambeth MS 131 folio side and column contains the Part I ending at Furnivall line 16,688?

next_steps: Obtain a folio-specific catalog description or inspect fol. 4; do not inherit aggregate dates from the composite MARCO record.
Keep the circa-1861 guardbook assembly date separate from the medieval fragment.
openQuestions.question: What is the production date of the target fragment on Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4?

next_steps: Obtain a direct catalog entry, transcription, or image before assigning the fragment to the target lineage.
openQuestions.question: Does Gloucester Cathedral Library MS 36, fragment 8 overlap Part I, Part II, or both?

next_steps: Consult the Inner Temple manuscript catalog or a direct codicological description before adding a normalized date.
openQuestions.question: What production date should be assigned to Petyt 511.7 from a directly reviewable current catalog or codicological study?

next_steps: Seek a repository-owner clarification or file-level provenance statement before treating the Unlicense as an XML redistribution license.
Continue to separate XML-file rights from the medieval text and U-M item rights statement.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's Unlicense file explicitly cover inherited XML encodings such as ahb1379?

next_steps: Use the Library's photography-order process for required leaves and seek separate reuse permission before redistribution.
Do not describe the five-image LUNA selection as a whole-manuscript facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete digital facsimile of Lambeth MS 131 be obtained or commissioned?

next_steps: Request target fol. 4 from Bodleian imaging services if direct paleographical verification is needed.
Keep the ten-canvas public IIIF object marked target absent.
openQuestions.question: Can a public or requestable image of Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4 be obtained?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for item-level digitization and OCR production records, including the physical-copy provenance of each volume.
Verify a human-usable complete image sequence or package before upgrading access:umich-page-images from unknown.
Keep the U-M image layer distinct from both Internet Archive scan carriers unless copy identity is directly established.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copies supplied the 880-image U-M layer, what item-level OCR or correction history applies beyond the generic header, and does a current complete page-image package or direct delivery route still exist?

next_steps: Apply to Inner Temple Library for direct manuscript access or current photography options.
Ask about access to the 1978 World Microfilms copy and verify the reported Bentley Historical Library MED Records Box 70 photostat directly.
Establish reuse terms separately; do not treat either collateral reproduction as Furnivall's source intermediary.

reviewNotes: The target is one bounded Part I work through printed line 16,688, not the entire two-part DIMEV record, the whole Furnivall publication, or all BODY content in the U-M encoding.
Furnivall's two physical 1887 volumes are modeled as separate edition expressions, and each Internet Archive scan is a separate facsimile carrier.

The Lambeth five-image IIIF object is complete only as a provider selection; it is partial coverage of MS 131 and of the target. The Rawlinson ten-canvas IIIF object is complete as a provider selection but omits target fol. 4 entirely.

Access, copyright status, licenses, and provider reproduction conditions are separate assertions. The U-M NoC-US designation is not conflated with the legacy XML AVAILABILITY warning or with the unresolved scope of the CME repository's Unlicense file.
No date more precise than the directly supported source permits is assigned to Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4, and no exact Part I overlap is assigned to Gloucester fragment 8.

The current Medictionary XML is a distinct structural version of the U-M-derived encoding: its BODY text is identical after whitespace normalization and its core BODY/LG/L/PB/MILESTONE counts agree, but it changes the document wrapper, note and emphasis element names, front-matter modeling, attributes, and availability statement.

lineage.summary: The checked-in artifact is the pinned CME copy of U-M item AHB1379.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR encoding with 880 linked page-image references made from Frederick James Furnivall's two separate 1887 volume expressions. Furnivall's first-part text ends at printed line 16,688. He takes lines 1-198 and a finite set of later main-text supplies from Inner Temple Petyt 511.7, uses Lambeth Palace Library MS 131 as the principal base from line 199 with those supplies excluded, and collates Petyt throughout. Bodleian Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4 independently preserves target lines 13,018-13,193. Merton 23.b.6 is explicitly Part II; Gloucester Cathedral MS 36 fragment 8 is not assigned to Part I without a direct scope. The two Internet Archive scans, U-M page-image layer, five-image Lambeth selection, and ten-canvas Rawlinson object are distinct access and facsimile layers.

catalogSummary.title: The Chronicle of England, Part 1

catalogingSubject.label: The Chronicle of England, Part 1
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual work is Robert Mannyng of Brunne's first chronicle part, ending at Furnivall's printed line 16,688. It is one Middle English verse work, not the whole two-part chronicle, not Furnivall's two-volume 1887 publication, and not the complete U-M/CME encoded item. The separately modeled representation context includes the forty-two-line transition into Part II (printed lines 16,689-16,730), Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus, and zero-count front and back matter.

assertion.notes: The date belongs to the medieval chronicle, not to the surviving manuscript copies, the 1887 edition, or the 2006 encoding.

extent.description: Raw representation accounting for the complete encoded item: 281 top-level DIV1 containers (FRONT 2, BODY 278, BACK 1), 436 LG and 16,974 L. The cataloged intellectual target is one verse work through printed line 16,688 and accounts for 431 direct BODY LG and 16,587 direct BODY L. Direct BODY representation totals 432 LG and 16,629 L after adding the one-LG, forty-two-line Part II transition. Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus adds four LG and 345 L, producing the raw 436/16,974 totals; front and back matter add no LG or L. Furnivall's terminal printed label is 16,730, which implies 16,630 numbered positions because labels jump from 12,899 to 13,000. The XML nevertheless has 16,629 direct BODY L because printed p. 360 labels 10,296-10,309 span thirteen encoded rows rather than fourteen; this is a numbering anomaly, not a missing encoded row. Item and form counts describe the one intellectual target, while section/LG/L totals intentionally describe the containing representation.

formStatements.description: A verse chronicle in rhyming couplets. A brief direct BODY prose paragraph containing a Latin antiphon, editorial NOTE1 prose, rubrics, and display conventions do not make the one count-bearing medieval work a mixed-form item.

assertion.notes: The U-M header's generic eng is normalized to enm for discovery.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief embedded Latin and modern English editorial material do not make the medieval work bilingual or mixed-language for discovery.
The one-item boundary ends at printed line 16,688. The following forty-two direct BODY lines are explicitly represented but never counted as part of ahb1379.
The extent deliberately reports both intellectual counts and raw encoding counts. Raw LG/L totals are not treated as proof of additional works.

Only Inner Temple Petyt 511.7, Lambeth Palace Library MS 131, and Bodleian Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4 are bound as secure witnesses to the bounded Part I target. Merton 23.b.6 is explicitly Part II, and Gloucester fragment 8 remains unscoped.
The discovery language is enm only. Scoped Latin and modern editorial English are preserved in language statements without labeling the work bilingual or mixed-language.

next_steps: Review current Mannyng scholarship before changing the associated-place statement into a place-of-composition assertion.
openQuestions.question: Can the exact place of composition be established more narrowly than the supported Sixhills/Lincolnshire authorial context?

next_steps: Obtain a direct catalog description or images of fragment 8 before treating it as a Part I source witness.
openQuestions.question: Does Gloucester Cathedral Library MS 36, fragment 8 overlap the bounded Part I target, and if so at what exact lines?

assertion.notes: Sixhills is retained as supported authorial and compositional context; the draft does not convert that context into an exact place-of-composition assertion.
Translation chiefly describes Mannyng's use of Wace; it does not imply line-for-line equivalence.
This is responsibility for the 1887 source edition, not medieval authorship.

subjects.label: Great Britain -- History
subjects.scheme: U-M source subject normalized as a General Corpus tag

summaries.value: Robert Mannyng's first chronicle part recounts legendary and early British history in Middle English rhyming couplets, chiefly adapting Wace's Roman de Brut while drawing selectively on other historical authorities. The bounded work ends with the six-line Latin epitaph through Furnivall line 16,688; the ensuing forty-two lines announce and begin Part II and are not counted as part of this work.

The intellectual target is printed lines 1-16,688 on pp. 1-579. Printed lines 16,689-16,730 on pp. 579-580 are a transition into Part II. Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus contributes 345 encoded L elements in four LG elements but no additional work item; front and back matter likewise contribute no work items.

assertion.notes: This supplied title distinguishes the bounded first part from titles used for the complete two-part chronicle and for Furnivall's publication.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloger, following MEC/MED BIB2350's Part 1 title and Furnivall's explicit boundary

notes: The source title is not used as proof that the two parts constitute one cataloging unit.
